serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Alleppey Prawn Curry weighs approximately 350 g and is typically served as a main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn Curry (coconut-based) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) used for cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Alleppey prawn curry is a traditional Kerala dish. ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides the closest approved primary source for Indian prawn curry preparations. Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against ICMR-NIN prawn curry data and USDA FDC SR Legacy shrimp (cooked) data. The input values are broadly consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for coconut-milk prawn curry (energy ~130–145 kcal/100g, protein ~9–11g, fat ~8–9g, carbohydrate ~5–6g). Input values retained with medium confidence and documented as adapted from ICMR-NIN nearest match. Sodium 271 mg/100g is plausible for a salted curry preparation. Saturated fat 5.71g/100g is consistent with coconut milk content.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on mixed seafood curry with coconut milk and low-carbohydrate vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Alleppey Prawn Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood curry with coconut milk and low-carbohydrate vegetables from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.71g/100g, primarily from raw mango and onion; coconut milk fat significantly blunts glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 350g serving = (45 × 5.71 × 3.5) / 100 = 8.99 ≈ 9.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100g — borderline for a curry dish; a 350g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, approaching 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced added salt during cooking and choose light coconut milk to limit both sodium and saturated fat, which contributes to arterial stiffness; pairing with potassium-rich foods such as leafy greens can help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate at 5.71g/100g with high protein at 10g/100g; fat from coconut milk slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise; estimated GI 45 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Alleppey Prawn Curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served with a low-GI carbohydrate such as brown rice or cauliflower rice; the high protein and fat content significantly attenuate the glycaemic response, but saturated fat from coconut milk should be moderated to avoid worsening insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.71g/100g from coconut milk; a 350g serving delivers approximately 20g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20g for adults; cholesterol 71.43mg/100g from prawns adds further consideration.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200g and substitute full-fat coconut milk with light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat intake; prawns provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, but the coconut milk base elevates LDL-raising saturated fat to a clinically significant level at standard serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.71g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; coconut milk saturated fat promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute full-fat coconut milk with light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat below 5g/100g; turmeric and ginger in the recipe provide beneficial anti-inflammatory polyphenols and should be retained; nightshade-sensitive individuals should request omission of green chillies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Alleppey Prawn Curry (also spelled Allepey Prawn Curry or Alappuzha Prawn Curry) is a traditional South Indian seafood dish originating from the coastal town of Alleppey (Alappuzha) in Kerala, India. It is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish features succulent tiger prawns simmered in a rich, tangy coconut milk gravy flavoured with raw mango, shredded ginger, turmeric, green chillies, fenugreek seeds, curry leaves, and mustard seeds. The cooking method is slow stewing, which allows the prawns to absorb the complex spice profile while the coconut milk creates a velvety, mildly sweet and sour sauce. The texture is tender and juicy with a creamy, aromatic gravy. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 137 kcal, 10g protein, 8.6g fat (5.7g saturated from coconut milk), and only 5.7g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-protein dish. Sodium is 271 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertensive individuals at a standard 350g serving. The dish is naturally gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (estimated 45) and high protein content, but the saturated fat from coconut milk warrants moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. It is best served with steamed rice or appam.

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source. Values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN prawn curry data and USDA FDC SR Legacy cooked shrimp data and found to be broadly consistent. No exact approved-source match exists for Alleppey Prawn Curry as a named dish; the record is classified as adapted/estimated from the closest ICMR-NIN Indian prawn curry entry. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional Kerala prawn curry preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as a named dish; only total fat and saturated fat are reported. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not available in the approved sources consulted; estimated ~280mg/100g from ingredient composition (prawns, onion, coconut milk) but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific traditional preparation not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FDC as a named composite dish. 4. GI estimated at 45 (low) from analogous mixed seafood coconut curry per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables; no direct GI measurement exists for Alleppey Prawn Curry. Glycemic load per 350g serving = (45 × 5.71 × 3.5) / 100 = 9.0. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is not an approved primary nutrient source; it has been replaced with ICMR-NIN and USDA FDC as the approved evidence basis. 6. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Bombay Curry And Kebabs North Indian Cuisine' (a restaurant name) to 'South Indian / Keralan' reflecting the dish's actual culinary origin. 7. trans_fat_g 0.03g/100g retained from input; this trace level is plausible from minor industrial processing of coconut milk and is not naturally zero — value is a trace amount consistent with processed coconut milk products.
